justification: Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions (CA mandatory, DE works council blocking, ID optional) — pass criterion met. Germany works council identified as a hard pre-condition (BR-3, AC-2, rejection criteria) — pass criterion met. Driver classification data model difference identified (BR-7, employee vs contractor per jurisdiction) — pass criterion met. No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific — pass criterion met. However, confidence.level is 82%, far exceeding the ≤60 threshold required for pass (and exceeding 75 for partial, hitting the ≥80 fail criterion). Additionally, there is no formal escalation stating implementation cannot proceed without legal review — the output identifies legal/compliance as stakeholders but proceeds to deliver full acceptance criteria rather than escalating with 'Cannot produce a single acceptance criteria set' and blocking on legal review. These two failures (overconfident, no escalation) prevent a pass despite otherwise excellent domain analysis.

observations.text: All 3 jurisdictions analyzed with correct rule identification: CA pay transparency as regulatory invariant, DE works council as blocking pre-condition, ID as optional
Works council correctly identified as having co-determination rights with legal blocking power under BetrVG §87, not advisory
Driver classification difference (employee vs contractor) correctly identified as jurisdiction-bound data model concern in BR-7
Rejection criteria explicitly forbids single global feature flag — demonstrates understanding that uniform rollout is impossible
Subtle trap about classification misapplication partially surfaced — rejection criteria warns against defaulting to contractor when not explicitly set
Cross-border edge case identified (DE/NL driver) with correct per-delivery jurisdiction determination
Confidence at 82% is far too high for multi-jurisdiction legal complexity — rubric requires ≤60 for pass, and ≥80 is a fail criterion
No formal escalation for legal review — Bird should have stated it cannot produce final acceptance criteria without legal/HR review of CA and DE requirements, instead of delivering a complete AC set
Did not explicitly surface the subtle trap that removing the feature in CA would be illegal while removing it in ID would be fine — the asymmetric removal risk is not called out

agent_output_excerpt:
## Domain Analysis — Driver Earnings Transparency

### Business Context

This feature encodes the business process of **compensation disclosure to drivers** — surfacing how each delivery's earnings are composed (base pay, bonuses, tips, deductions) in real time. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **regulated compensation process** that intersects labor law, worker classification, and institutional approval rights across jurisdictions.
